translation: Setup.We consider a request scheduling problem in LLM serving for agentic workflows,where the system must determine request-to-GPU assignment and queuing order within each GPU’s local queue before each round of request p
info_points: Setup.We consider a request scheduling problem in LLM serving for agentic workflows,where the system must determine request-to-GPU assignment and queuing order within each GPU’s local queue before each round of request processing.Unlike the previous scenarios that operate at cluster-level deployment
source_text: Setup.We consider a request scheduling problem in LLM serving for agentic workflows,where the system must determine request-to-GPU assignment and queuing order within each GPU’s local queue before each round of request processing.Unlike the previous scenarios that operate at cluster-level deployment granularity, schedulingdecisionsherearemaderoundbyround,andtheobjectiveistominimizeend-to-endrequest processing time.The core trade-off is therefore direct:Scheduling computation time competes with request completion time.

translation: Trace.We use two non-overlapping 64-workflow slices subsampled from ShareGPT [39].Each workflow is a multi-call agentic request trace with online call revelation.Detailed scheduling problem formulation and trace informat
info_points: Trace.We use two non-overlapping 64-workflow slices subsampled from ShareGPT [39].Each workflow is a multi-call agentic request trace with online call revelation.Detailed scheduling problem formulation and trace information are provided in Appendix C and Appendix H.
source_text: Trace.We use two non-overlapping 64-workflow slices subsampled from ShareGPT [39].Each workflow is a multi-call agentic request trace with online call revelation.Detailed scheduling problem formulation and trace information are provided in Appendix C and Appendix H.

translation: Baselines.We compare against two static baselines:An MILP-based policy that solves the request assignment and queuing problem optimally but incurs high per-round scheduling overhead, and a greedy-based policy that produc
info_points: Baselines.We compare against two static baselines:An MILP-based policy that solves the request assignment and queuing problem optimally but incurs high per-round scheduling overhead, and a greedy-based policy that produces assignments quickly but struggles to achieve optimal request completion time.
source_text: Baselines.We compare against two static baselines:An MILP-based policy that solves the request assignment and queuing problem optimally but incurs high per-round scheduling overhead, and a greedy-based policy that produces assignments quickly but struggles to achieve optimal request completion time.Table 4 summarizes the results.

source_text: Executionmodelcalibration.Asthisscenariooperatesattherequestlevelratherthantheclusterlevel,no model migration or parallelism reconfiguration occurs between rounds.The execution model (§5.1) naturally accommodatesthis by eliminatingthe reconfigurationcomponent,reducingTtotaltothe sum ofper-round scheduling overhead and serving time (i.e., Σtsched + Σtserve).

translation: Results analysis.The evolved policy outperforms all baselines on both traces.On Trace 1, the greedy-based policies maintain negligible scheduling overhead but produce weaker serving plans, yielding Ttotalof 10.5s.
info_points: Results analysis.The evolved policy outperforms all baselines on both traces.On Trace 1, the greedy-based policies maintain negligible scheduling overhead but produce weaker serving plans, yielding Ttotalof 10.5s.
source_text: Results analysis.The evolved policy outperforms all baselines on both traces.On Trace 1, the greedy-based policies maintain negligible scheduling overhead but produce weaker serving plans, yielding Ttotalof 10.5s.

source_text: Trace 1 Trace 2 Policy Σtsched Σtserve Ttotal Σtsched Σtserve Ttotal Greedy 0.1s 10.4s 10.5s 0.1s 18.0s 18.1s MILP 6.9s 25.0s 31.9s 2.2s 10.3s 12.5s Ours 1.3s 5.6s 6.8s 0.4s 6.7s 7.1s TheMILP-basedpolicy optimizeseach roundexactly butbecomes scheduling-dominated:Its accumulated scheduling overhead reaches 6.9s, inflating Ttotalto 31.9s.In contrast, the evolved policy reduces scheduling time to 1.3s while simultaneously improving serving quality, achieving a Ttotalof 6.8s (35% reduction over the greedy baseline and 79% over the MILP-based policy).A similar trend holds on the harder Trace 2, where the evolved policy achieves Ttotalof 7.1s, outperforming the greedy-based (18.1s, 61% reduction) and MILP-based (12.5s, 43% reduction) policies.

source_text: Insight 4: Our LLM-driven program synthesis workflow adapts across serving scenarios.The LLM-driven program synthesisworkflow(§5.3)adaptstodifferenttypesofruntimedynamics(fromcluster-leveldeploymenttorequest- levelscheduling)bytailoringtheexecutionmodel(§5.1)tothesystemcoststructureofeachscenario.Inrequest scheduling, the evolved policy synthesizes domain-specific principles (e.g., priority-aware queuing, load-aware affinity) thatapproximateoptimalsolutionsatafractionofthecomputationalcost,demonstratingourworkflow’sabilityto discover efficient operating points in diverse trade-off spaces and serving scenarios.

source_text: WepresentAUTOPOIESIS,aself-evolvingservingparadigmthatshiftsLLMservingfromstatic,human- engineered policy design to continuous, LLM-driven policy evolution.By decoupling policy execution from policyevolutionthroughatwo-planearchitecture,AUTOPOIESIScontinuouslyobservesruntimedynamics, evolves serving policies via LLM-driven program synthesis, and deploys superior policies without disrupting liveserving.Evaluationsacrossdifferentservingscenariosdemonstrateupto53%andonaverage34% improvementoverstate-of-the-artservingsystems.WebelieveAUTOPOIESISestablishesanewparadigm inwhichservingpoliciesarenolongerfixedartifactsbutlivingcodethatevolvesalongsidetheruntime conditions.
